title: Signal Dry Bulk Weekly Report
summary:
Chart of the Week : Dry Bulk Congestion Brazil Panamax
Higher monthly volumes of congestion at major Brazilian ports and days waiting
In the third week of November, the larger ship categories recorded greater stability in freight rates, which indicates a more solid development. In contrast, a downward trend was observed in the smaller ship sizes, particularly in the Handysize category. At the beginning of the fourth quarter, the stability of the upturn in freight rates is being called into question.

children.title: Chart of the Week : Dry Bulk Congestion Brazil Panamax
section_path: Chart of the Week : Dry Bulk Congestion Brazil Panamax
children.section_path_text: Chart of the Week : Dry Bulk Congestion Brazil Panamax
children.summary:
Higher monthly volumes of congestion at major Brazilian ports and days waiting
Image reference: unnamed - 2023-11-16T122731.810.png | assets/2023-11-16_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_unnamed-2023-11-16t122731810_906ea8c4433e.png
In the third week of November, the larger ship categories recorded greater stability in freight rates, which indicates a more solid development. In contrast, a downward trend was observed in the smaller ship sizes, particularly in the Handysize category.

children.title: SECTION 1/ FREIGHT
children.summary:
‘The Big Picture' - Capesize and Panamax Bulkers and Smaller Ship Sizes Market Rates ($/t) Mixed
Image reference: unnamed - 2023-11-16T122852.758.png | assets/2023-11-16_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_unnamed-2023-11-16t122852758_968755cb9c50.png
The third week of November began with mixed sentiment on freight rates, as the larger vessel categories seemed to hold strength compared to the downward pressure in Handysize and Supramax. Capesize vessel freight rates from Brazil to North China rose to $19.6/tonne, with the last low recorded in mid-September.

children.title: SECTION 2/ SUPPLY
children.summary:
Supply Trend Lines for Key Load Areas Ballasters (#vessels) Increasing
Image reference: unnamed - 2023-11-16T123053.362.png | assets/2023-11-16_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_unnamed-2023-11-16t123053362_23c205cba14d.png
In the third week of November, there was an increase in the number of ballasters with the Capesize and Supramax size segments showing a higher trend than other ship sizes. Capesize SE Africa: The number of ballasters kept rising above the annual average of 100, with the last low in week 40.

children.title: SECTION 3/ DEMAND
children.summary:
Summary of Dry Bulk Demand, per Ship Size Tonne Days Decreasing
Image reference: unnamed - 2023-11-16T123140.065.png | assets/2023-11-16_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_unnamed-2023-11-16t123140065_ef8021672323.png
Demand growth was down for all ship sizes and there were no signs of recovery yet, with a final upturn in week 42. Capesize: The downward trend remained at a level that had not been reached before the last peak in week 41, while the decline does not seem to have stopped by the end of the month.

children.title: SECTION 4/ PORT CONGESTION
children.summary:
Dry bulk ships congested at Chinese ports No of Vessels Decreasing
Image reference: unnamed - 2023-11-16T123302.077.png | assets/2023-11-16_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_unnamed-2023-11-16t123302077_626b5ee983cf.png
The level of ship congestion decreased following the spike of the previous week, with lower levels of congestion in the large vessel size categories. Capesize: The number of congested vessels has fallen to 107, 7 fewer than the previous week, and it remains to be seen whether the number of overloaded ships will fall below 100.
